figures.caption: Table 4: Main speedup cells. Rows use different denominators; C-CEILING explains why they do not multiply.
Table 23: Claim/protocol/evidence manifest for the main quantitative assets (continued).

Table 9: Dense-answer-anchored C-PERSIST stress. Each follow-up prompt includes the previous canonical dense answer and the dense/cached arms receive identical prompt hashes. This is a content-conditioned prompt-variation stress, not natural dialogue. Parenthesized speedups in the median-follow-up column use an approximate 80 s cold-dense reference for readability.
Table 16: Paired-drift examples. Aggregate accuracy can preserve a point estimate while answer identity, correctness on individual examples, or output format changes.

Figure 1: Graphical overview. Video state is reused when validation gates allow it and refreshed when the scene, query, or cache topology requires it. C-PERSIST, C-CEILING, and C-VISION are the main regimes; candidate C-STREAM remains a state-update target.

Figure 6: Qwen routing-budget visualization on real clips. Panel A overlays static, shifted, and fresh block classes for one transition, then reports reused versus fresh active-region budget; static/shifted blocks are reused until age four, while novel or expired blocks are fresh. Panel B applies the same policy to three short windows: a high-reuse TOMATO example, a VideoMME visual anchor, and a lower-reuse boundary. These rows visualize the routing budget, not additional benchmark-frontier points or speedup claims.
Table 3: Main evaluation lanes. The rows are protocol identifiers, not a claim that all results share one denominator.

Table 8: Many-turn C-PERSIST stress on seven 20f short VideoMME videos. The stress cycles the same three questions through a 50-turn stateless question schedule, so it tests cache-horizon stability under repeated follow-ups rather than natural dialogue. The 560 dense baseline rows are deterministic replicas of 21 unique stateless dense runs for turn-matched pairing, not independent timing samples.
Table 1: Reuse regime map. Rows are separate workloads and denominators, not one multiplier.

Figure 4: C-CEILING share-model validation. Predicted speedup uses dense vision share and pruned-run vision reduction, then compares with independently observed end-to-end speedup. The n=60 composition audit and low-share Qwen point show denominator binding; measured-sparse markers add Qwen timing-validating fidelity failures plus the low-gain 16f boundary. The clean measured sparse-execution headline is Gemma 32f short in Table 4.

Table 12: Measured sparse vision execution on Gemma. The timed path skips real vision-tower work. Full-sweep rows preserve paired accuracy and agreement but share parse failures; only 32f short is parse-clean and supplies the 1.316× headline cell.
Figure 5: Qwen base-policy routing frontier under dense-backend substitution.
Table 20: How observed limitations motivate future media-interface requirements. These are hypotheses and candidate design requirements, not measured claims in this paper.

Table 5: Selective re-prefill repair frontier. Fixed K = 1 is the no-coordination baseline; adaptive repaired-cache inheritance is the broad repair policy. Gains use cold/repaired follow-up or all-query denominators; setup-inclusive economics are in Table 10.

Table 13: Measured sparse vision execution on Qwen. The 8f sweep validates C-CEILING timing but has no fidelity-preserving, speed-positive point. The 16f sweep recovers to within the aggregateaccuracy/format gates at krV = 0.85, but not paired identity and not the vision-reduction gate.

Table 14: Numeric residuals for the original C-CEILING cells. The prediction is computed from measured share terms, not fit to the observed end-to-end multiplier. The newer measured sparseexecution rows are plotted in Figure 4 and tabulated in Tables 12 and 13. Residual is observed minus predicted speedup. MVBench’s advisory residual is not cleanly attributable, so it is not used as a clean first-pass headline.
Table 22: Architecture lessons from the tested evidence. This is not a model leaderboard; it is a runtime-interface lesson about which reuse claims can be tested cleanly.

Figure 2: Unrepaired persistent-KV frame-scaling envelope. The figure separates the raw warmreuse probe from the repaired C-PERSIST breadth result in Table 5: 7B stays inside the tested raw envelope through 16 frames / 6.5k prefill tokens, while 3B trades paired fidelity for a wider pre-basin plateau through 36 frames / 14.5k prefill tokens.
Table 19: Application regimes as evidence targets, not generic motivation. The right policy depends on the redundancy structure and on what failure would cost.
Table 2: Runtime hooks and validation gates required to turn each reuse mechanism into a systems claim without confusing semantic reuse, real skipped work, and correctness.

Table 15: Qwen composition boundary. The tested admission family has no deployable composition point: cache reuse and invalidation hit the same aggregate loss through different drift sets, and only cache reuse preserves the three-query speed profile. Speedup is paired three-query E2E versus cold all-query execution; follow-up vision active is the measured activity fraction; krQ0 is first-query visual-token keep rate.

Table 10: Setup-inclusive same-video follow-up economics. Warm is mean per-follow-up speedup; Q = k is session speedup for k same-video queries. Qwen counts one measured cold first query plus Q − 1 C-PERSIST follow-ups; Gemma counts prefix warm-up plus Q follow-ups for denominator comparison. ∆acc is paired session-vs-baseline accuracy delta where available; Gemma zeros reflect zero paired correctness diffs.

Figure 3: Adaptive C-PERSIST timing attribution. Fixed K = 1 buys the newest-frame tail again at the second follow-up; adaptive repair mostly appends text from the repaired cache. The visual shows the short-slice timing mechanism behind the 9.50× paired second-follow-up speedup; the broader 0/93 fidelity result is in Table 5.
